1:
"This is the third time I am coming to you. In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established." The brief verse notes the third coming and the rule of witnesses. The grammatical voices note the announcement. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states "this is the third time I am coming to you" — his third intended visit — and cites the legal rule (Deuteronomy 19:15): "in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established."

On the rule of witnesses, the witnesses note its application. Barnes and Calvin note Paul applies the Mosaic principle to church discipline: he will proceed against offenders not rashly but by due process, on the testimony of two or three witnesses; every charge must be duly established.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the warning: by citing this rule, Paul signals that on his coming he will hold a formal inquiry and act judicially against the impenitent; his discipline will be orderly and just, not arbitrary. The verse displays the third coming and the rule of witnesses.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul states "this is the third time I am coming to you" and cites the legal rule (Deuteronomy 19:15), "in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established" (Barnes, Robertson); applying the Mosaic principle to church discipline — he will proceed against offenders by due process, on proper testimony (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Henry note he signals a formal, just inquiry on his coming. The verse displays the rule of witnesses. The verse marks the coming: the third time. The verse displays the rule: two or three witnesses. The verse displays the application: church discipline. The verse displays the warning: judicial inquiry. The rule displays Paul's discipline to be orderly and just. The verse displays the third visit: announced. The verse displays the witnesses: required. The verse displays the process: due. The third coming and the rule of witnesses display Paul's resolve to act justly: announcing his third visit, he cites the Mosaic rule of two or three witnesses, signaling that he will deal with the impenitent not arbitrarily but by orderly, judicial process on duly established testimony. The verse displays the apostle's just discipline, with a solemn forewarning (v.2).
2:
"I told you before, and foretell you, as if I were present, the second time; and being absent now I write to them which heretofore have sinned, and to all other, that, if I come again, I will not spare:" The brief verse notes Paul's solemn warning that he will not spare the impenitent. The grammatical voices note the forewarning. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "told them before" (on his second visit) and now "foretells (forewarns)" again, "as if present" though absent, addressing "them which heretofore have sinned, and all other" — giving plain warning to the guilty.

On "if I come again, I will not spare," the witnesses note the threat of discipline. Barnes and Calvin note the solemn declaration: "if I come again, I will not spare" — he will no longer be lenient with the impenitent, but will exercise his apostolic authority in discipline; his forbearance has a limit.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the mercy in the warning: Paul gives this forewarning precisely so that they may repent and he may not have to act; the threat is meant to bring them to amendment before he comes. The verse displays Paul's solemn warning that he will not spare the impenitent.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "told them before" and now "foretells again, as if present" though absent, warning "them which heretofore have sinned and all other," giving plain warning to the guilty (Barnes, Robertson); the solemn declaration "if I come again, I will not spare," no longer lenient but exercising discipline, his forbearance having a limit (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the warning is meant to bring repentance before he comes. The verse displays the warning not to spare. The verse marks the forewarning: told before, foretell again. The verse displays the threat: will not spare. The verse displays the limit: of forbearance. The verse displays the mercy: that they repent. The warning displays Paul's leniency ending for the impenitent. The verse displays the foretelling: plain. The verse displays the sparing: withheld. The verse displays the purpose: amendment. Paul's solemn warning that he will not spare the impenitent displays the limit of his forbearance: having warned them before and now again, he declares that on his coming he will no longer be lenient but will discipline the guilty — yet the very forewarning is mercy, meant to bring them to repentance before he must act. The verse displays the apostle's resolved discipline, backed by the proof of Christ in him (vv.3-4).
3:
"Since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you-ward is not weak, but is mighty in you." The brief verse notes the proof of Christ speaking in Paul. The grammatical voices note the demand answered. Barnes and Robertson note Paul takes up the Corinthians' own demand: "since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me" — they wanted evidence that Christ truly spoke and acted through him — he will give it, in his coming discipline.

On "not weak, but mighty in you," the witnesses note the power. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ, speaking in Paul, is "not weak toward you, but mighty in you" — far from the weakness his critics alleged, Christ's power had been displayed among them (in their conversion, the miracles, the gifts) and would be displayed again in discipline.

The Reformed Calvin notes the warning: if they demand proof of Christ's power in Paul, they will find it — but in a way they may not wish, in the exercise of judgment against the impenitent. The verse displays the proof of Christ speaking in Paul.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul takes up their demand — "since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me" — and will give it in his coming discipline (Barnes, Robertson); Christ in Paul being "not weak toward you, but mighty in you," his power displayed among them in conversion, miracles, and gifts, and to be displayed again (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes they will find the proof, but in judgment. The verse displays the proof of Christ in Paul. The verse marks the demand: a proof sought. The verse displays the power: not weak but mighty. The verse displays the display: among them. The verse displays the warning: proof in judgment. The proof displays Christ's power in Paul to be shown in discipline. The verse displays the seeking: of proof. The verse displays the might: of Christ. The verse displays the warning: to the impenitent. The proof of Christ speaking in Paul displays his answer to their demand: since they seek evidence that Christ speaks through him, they will have it — for Christ in him is not weak but mighty, his power already shown among them and to be shown again in the discipline of the impenitent. The verse displays the might of Christ in the apostle, grounded in Christ's own death and life (v.4).
4:
"For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God toward you." The witnesses note the pattern of Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power. On "though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God," the grammatical voices note Christ's pattern. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds his argument in Christ: Christ "was crucified through (in) weakness" — submitting to the apparent weakness of the cross — "yet he liveth by the power of God," raised and reigning in divine power; weakness gave way to power.

On "we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note Paul applies it to himself: like his Master, he too is "weak in him" (sharing Christ's weakness, appearing lowly and suffering) "but shall live with him by the power of God toward you" — manifesting Christ's power in dealing with them.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the parallel: as Christ's weakness was followed by resurrection power, so Paul's apparent weakness will be followed by a powerful exercise of authority among the Corinthians; the servant follows the Master's pattern. The verse displays the pattern of Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power.

The witnesses agree: Paul grounds his argument in Christ, who "was crucified through (in) weakness" yet "liveth by the power of God," weakness giving way to resurrection power (Barnes, Robertson); applying it to himself — like his Master he is "weak in him" (sharing Christ's lowliness and suffering) "but shall live with him by the power of God toward you," manifesting Christ's power in dealing with them (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the servant follows the Master's pattern. The verse displays Christ's pattern of weakness and power. The verse marks the cross: weakness. The verse displays the resurrection: power of God. The verse displays the application: Paul weak then mighty. The verse displays the parallel: servant like Master. The pattern displays weakness followed by divine power in Christ and Paul. The verse displays the crucifixion: in weakness. The verse displays the living: by power. The verse displays the apostle: following the pattern. The pattern of Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power displays the ground of Paul's coming authority: as Christ submitted to the weakness of the cross but now lives by God's power, so Paul, weak with Christ in his lowly sufferings, will yet act among the Corinthians in the power of God. The verse displays the Master's pattern in the servant, calling them to self-examination (v.5).
5:
"Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates?" The witnesses note the call to self-examination, whether Christ is in them. On "examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves," the grammatical voices note the turn. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns the demand for proof back on them: instead of testing him, "examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith" — test and prove your own selves, your own genuineness as believers.

On "know ye not... how that Jesus Christ is in you," the witnesses note the test. Barnes and Calvin note the criterion: they should know that "Jesus Christ is in you" (by his Spirit, by faith, by the new life) — the indwelling Christ being the proof of true Christianity; this is the mark to look for in self-examination.

On "except ye be reprobates," the witnesses note the alternative. Barnes and Wesley note the solemn exception: "except ye be reprobates (adokimoi, disapproved, counterfeit, those that fail the test)" — unless they are false professors, Christ dwells in them; if he does not, they are reprobate. The verse displays the call to self-examination, whether Christ is in them.

The witnesses agree: Paul turns the demand for proof back on them — "examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves," testing their own genuineness (Barnes, Robertson); the criterion being that "Jesus Christ is in you" by his Spirit and faith, the indwelling Christ the proof of true Christianity (Barnes, Calvin); "except ye be reprobates (disapproved, counterfeit)" — unless false professors, Christ dwells in them (Barnes, Wesley). The verse displays self-examination. The verse marks the call: examine yourselves. The verse displays the test: Christ in you. The verse displays the proof: the indwelling Christ. The verse displays the exception: reprobates. The call displays the believers proving their own faith. The verse displays the examining: of self. The verse displays the Christ: indwelling. The verse displays the reprobate: who fails. The call to self-examination, whether Christ is in them, displays Paul's pointed turn: rather than testing him, the Corinthians should test themselves — whether they are truly in the faith, with Christ dwelling in them by his Spirit; for unless they are counterfeit believers, he does, and this is the true proof. The verse displays the inward test of genuine faith, which Paul trusts they will pass (v.6).
6:
"But I trust that ye shall know that we are not reprobates." The brief verse notes Paul's trust that they will know he is not a reprobate. The grammatical voices note the confidence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul expresses his "trust (hope, confidence)" that the Corinthians "shall know that we are not reprobates (disapproved, counterfeit)" — that he will not fail the test, but will be proved a true and approved apostle.

On the connection, the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Calvin note the link to v.5: if Christ is in them (as their conversion proves), then Paul, through whom they were converted, is no reprobate but a true minister; their genuineness vindicates his.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the gentleness: Paul hopes they will recognize his approval not by his exercising severe discipline but by the evident fruit of his ministry among them. The verse displays Paul's trust that they will know he is not a reprobate.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul expresses his "trust" that the Corinthians "shall know that we are not reprobates," that he will not fail the test but be proved a true apostle (Barnes, Robertson); the link to v.5 being that if Christ is in them, Paul, through whom they were converted, is no reprobate, their genuineness vindicating his (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes he hopes they recognize his approval by his fruit, not his severity. The verse displays Paul not a reprobate. The verse marks the trust: he is approved. The verse displays the proof: their conversion. The verse displays the link: their faith, his vindication. The verse displays the hope: recognized by fruit. The trust displays Paul's apostleship vindicated by their faith. The verse displays the knowing: that he is approved. The verse displays the reprobate: which he is not. The verse displays the proof: in them. Paul's trust that they will know he is not a reprobate displays the confidence of a true minister: he hopes the Corinthians will recognize his approval before God — for their own genuine faith, the fruit of his ministry, is itself the proof that he is no counterfeit but a true and approved apostle. The verse displays the apostle's vindication through his converts, his deeper concern being their good (v.7).
7:
"Now I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved, but that ye should do that which is honest, though we be as reprobates." The brief verse notes Paul's prayer for their goodness, not his own reputation. The grammatical voices note the selfless prayer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "prays to God that ye do no evil" — his desire is for their holiness — "not that we should appear approved" (not to vindicate his own reputation by their obedience) "but that ye should do that which is honest (good, honourable)."

On "though we be as reprobates," the witnesses note the self-abnegation. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's remarkable selflessness: he would rather they do right even if it meant he "be as a reprobate" (appear without proof of his power, having no occasion to display authority in discipline) — their good matters more to him than his own vindication.

The puritan Henry notes the spirit: a true minister cares more for his people's holiness than for his own credit; Paul would forgo the proof of his authority if only they would do well. The verse displays Paul's prayer for their goodness, not his own reputation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "prays to God that ye do no evil," desiring their holiness, "not that we should appear approved" (not to vindicate his reputation) "but that ye should do that which is honest (good)" (Barnes, Robertson); his selflessness — he would rather they do right even if he "be as a reprobate," lacking occasion to display his authority, their good mattering more than his vindication (Barnes, Calvin). Henry notes a true minister cares more for his people's holiness than his credit. The verse displays the selfless prayer. The verse marks the prayer: that ye do no evil. The verse displays the motive: not his reputation. The verse displays the desire: their good. The verse displays the selflessness: though he seem a reprobate. The prayer displays Paul valuing their holiness above his credit. The verse displays the praying: for them. The verse displays the good: their doing. The verse displays the self: set aside. Paul's prayer for their goodness, not his own reputation, displays the selflessness of true ministry: he prays that they do no evil but what is honourable — not so that his authority may be vindicated, but for their own good; he would gladly forgo all proof of his power if only they would do well. The verse displays the apostle's self-forgetting love, grounded in his service to the truth (v.8).
8:
"For we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth." The brief verse notes that Paul's power serves the truth, not against it. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's maxim: "we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth" — his apostolic power is given to support and advance the truth, not to oppose it; he can act only in its service.

On the connection, the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the link to v.7: if the Corinthians do well (live in the truth), Paul has no occasion and indeed no power to act against them in discipline; his authority operates only on behalf of the truth, against error and sin, never against righteousness.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: the minister's power is entirely subordinate to the truth; it can never be used to crush the obedient, but only to defend the truth and correct what opposes it. The verse displays that Paul's power serves the truth, not against it.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's maxim "we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth," his power given to support and advance the truth, not oppose it (Barnes, Robertson); the link to v.7 being that if they live in the truth, he has no occasion to discipline them, his authority operating only for the truth, against error and sin (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the minister's power is wholly subordinate to the truth. The verse displays power for the truth. The verse marks the principle: nothing against the truth. The verse displays the purpose: for the truth. The verse displays the application: no power against the obedient. The verse displays the comfort: power subordinate to truth. The principle displays Paul's authority serving only the truth. The verse displays the truth: served. The verse displays the power: for it. The verse displays the obedience: safe. That Paul's power serves the truth, not against it, displays the principle that governs his authority: his apostolic power can act only on behalf of the truth, never against it — so the obedient who live in the truth have nothing to fear from him, his power being exercised only against error and sin. The verse displays the truth-serving nature of the ministry, content even in apparent weakness (v.9).
9:
"For we are glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong: and this also we wish, even your perfection." The brief verse notes Paul's gladness in his weakness if they are strong, wishing their perfection. The grammatical voices note the gladness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "is glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong" — he rejoices if he appears weak (has no occasion to use stern authority) provided the Corinthians "are strong" (in faith and holiness); their strength is his joy, even at the cost of his own seeming weakness.

On "this also we wish, even your perfection," the witnesses note the desire. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's earnest wish: "your perfection (katartisis, complete restoration, full setting-right, maturity)" — that they may be thoroughly mended, restored to soundness and complete in every grace; this is his prayer for them.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the aim: Paul's whole desire is their spiritual completeness; he would gladly be thought weak if only they were perfected in Christ. The verse displays Paul's gladness in his weakness if they are strong.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "is glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong," rejoicing if he appears weak (has no occasion for stern authority) provided they are strong in faith and holiness (Barnes, Robertson); his earnest wish being "your perfection (complete restoration, maturity)," that they be thoroughly mended and complete in every grace (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes he would gladly be thought weak if only they were perfected. The verse displays gladness in their strength. The verse marks the gladness: weak if ye are strong. The verse displays the wish: your perfection. The verse displays the joy: their strength. The verse displays the aim: their completeness. The gladness displays Paul rejoicing in their strength above his own credit. The verse displays the weakness: gladly borne. The verse displays the strength: theirs. The verse displays the perfection: wished. Paul's gladness in his weakness if they are strong, wishing their perfection, displays the selflessness of his love: he rejoices to seem weak — having no need to exercise stern discipline — provided the Corinthians are strong in faith, and his earnest prayer is for their complete restoration and maturity in every grace. The verse displays the apostle's desire for their perfection, the very purpose of his authority (v.10).
10:
"Therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction." The brief verse notes Paul writing beforehand so as not to use sharpness when present. The grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note Paul explains why he writes this severe warning "being absent": it is "lest being present I should use sharpness (severity)" — he writes ahead so that they may amend, and he need not deal harshly when he comes.

On "the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction," the witnesses note the nature of his authority. Barnes and Calvin note the recurring principle: the power the Lord gave Paul is "for edification (building up), and not for destruction (tearing down)" — even his sharpness, if used, would aim to save and build, not to ruin.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the tenderness: Paul prefers to warn by letter and win their repentance, rather than wound by severe discipline in person; his authority is reluctant to strike. The verse displays Paul writing beforehand so as not to use sharpness when present.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul writes the severe warning "being absent" precisely "lest being present I should use sharpness (severity)," writing ahead that they may amend and spare him harshness (Barnes, Robertson); the power the Lord gave him being "for edification, not destruction," even his sharpness aiming to build, not ruin (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom notes he prefers to warn by letter and win repentance. The verse displays writing to avoid sharpness. The verse marks the purpose: lest use sharpness. The verse displays the means: writing beforehand. The verse displays the power: for edification. The verse displays the tenderness: reluctant to strike. The writing displays Paul warning ahead to spare severity. The verse displays the absence: the time to warn. The verse displays the sharpness: to be avoided. The verse displays the edification: the aim. Paul writing beforehand so as not to use sharpness when present displays the gracious aim of his authority: he sends the severe warning in advance precisely so that they may repent and he need not be harsh in person — for the power the Lord gave him is for building up, not tearing down. The verse displays the edifying purpose of his discipline, sealed by the closing exhortation (v.11).
11:
"Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace; and the God of love and peace shall be with you." The witnesses note the closing exhortation and the promise of the God of love and peace. On the series of exhortations, the grammatical voices note the parting charges. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's affectionate close: "farewell (rejoice). Be perfect (be perfected, mended, restored); be of good comfort (be comforted, exhorted); be of one mind (think the same, agree); live in peace" — a string of brief, earnest charges.

On "of one mind... live in peace," the witnesses note the unity. Barnes and Calvin note the special stress on harmony: to a church torn by faction, Paul urges agreement and peace — the healing of their divisions; unity and peace are the chief needs.

On "the God of love and peace shall be with you," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the promise: if they so live in love and peace, "the God of love and peace" — the author and giver of both — "shall be with them"; his presence follows where love and peace are pursued. The verse displays the closing exhortation and the promise of the God of love and peace.

The witnesses agree: Paul's affectionate close — "farewell (rejoice); be perfect (be mended, restored); be of good comfort; be of one mind (agree); live in peace" — a string of earnest charges (Barnes, Robertson); the special stress on harmony, urging a faction-torn church to agreement and the healing of divisions (Barnes, Calvin); and the promise that if they so live, "the God of love and peace shall be with them," his presence following where love and peace are pursued (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays the closing exhortation. The verse marks the charges: be perfect, of one mind, in peace. The verse displays the unity: harmony urged. The verse displays the promise: the God of love and peace. The verse displays the condition: where love is pursued. The exhortation displays Paul charging unity and peace with a promise. The verse displays the farewell: affectionate. The verse displays the oneness: of mind. The verse displays the God: of love and peace. The closing exhortation and the promise of the God of love and peace display Paul's parting charge to a divided church: he urges them to be restored, comforted, of one mind, and at peace — and promises that the God of love and peace will dwell with them if they so live. The verse displays the apostle's final pastoral plea, sealed with greetings and the benediction (vv.12-14).
12:
"Greet one another with an holy kiss." The brief verse notes the charge to greet with a holy kiss. The grammatical voices note the custom. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's familiar parting charge: "greet one another with an holy kiss" — the customary token of Christian love and fellowship in the early church (as in Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20), a kiss of peace.

On "holy," the witnesses note the character. Barnes and Ellicott note the kiss is "holy" (hagios) — pure, chaste, sacred — expressing genuine brotherly affection and unity among believers, distinguished from any worldly or impure greeting.

The grammatical-historical note the fitness: coming after the call to be of one mind and live in peace (v.11), the holy kiss was the visible sign of the reconciliation and mutual love Paul urged on the divided Corinthians. The verse displays the charge to greet with a holy kiss.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's parting charge "greet one another with an holy kiss," the customary token of Christian love and fellowship (Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20), a kiss of peace (Barnes, Robertson); the kiss "holy" (pure, chaste, sacred), expressing genuine brotherly affection, distinguished from worldly greeting (Barnes, Ellicott). The grammatical-historical note it was the visible sign of the reconciliation Paul urged. The verse displays the holy kiss. The verse marks the charge: greet with a kiss. The verse displays the custom: token of fellowship. The verse displays the character: holy, pure. The verse displays the fitness: sign of reconciliation. The charge displays the holy kiss as the sign of brotherly love. The verse displays the greeting: mutual. The verse displays the kiss: holy. The verse displays the love: brotherly. The charge to greet with a holy kiss displays the visible token of the love Paul urged: the believers are to greet one another with the holy kiss — the pure, sacred sign of Christian fellowship and, for this divided church, of the reconciliation and mutual affection he has pressed upon them. The verse displays the sign of brotherly love, joined by the greeting of all the saints (v.13).
13:
"All the saints salute you." The brief verse notes the greeting of all the saints to the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul conveys the greeting of "all the saints" — all the believers where he was (probably Macedonia) — who "salute (greet) you"; a token of the universal fellowship of the church.

On "all the saints," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Ellicott note the comprehensiveness: not a few individuals but "all the saints" join in the greeting — the whole company of believers sending their love to the Corinthians, expressing the bond uniting the churches.

The grammatical-historical note the comfort: such greetings reminded the divided and troubled Corinthians that they were part of the wider family of God, loved and remembered by believers elsewhere. The verse displays the greeting of all the saints to the Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul conveys the greeting of "all the saints" — the believers where he was — who "salute (greet) you," a token of the church's universal fellowship (Barnes, Robertson); the comprehensiveness, "all the saints" joining, the whole company sending love and expressing the bond uniting the churches (Barnes, Ellicott). The grammatical-historical note this reminded the troubled Corinthians of the wider family of God. The verse displays the saints' greeting. The verse marks the salutation: all the saints. The verse displays the breadth: the whole company. The verse displays the bond: uniting the churches. The verse displays the comfort: the wider family. The greeting displays the universal fellowship of the church. The verse displays the saints: greeting. The verse displays the salute: to Corinth. The verse displays the fellowship: shared. The greeting of all the saints to the Corinthians displays the universal fellowship of the church: the whole company of believers where Paul was joins in saluting them — reminding the divided Corinthians that they belong to the wider family of God, loved and remembered by the saints elsewhere. The verse displays the communion of the saints, crowned by the trinitarian benediction (v.14). The brief greeting is more than a formality, for it sets the troubled and divided church of Corinth within the great fellowship of all the saints, reminding them that the love which they were failing to show one another was the very bond by which the whole body of believers, scattered through many cities, was knit together in Christ.
14:
"The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the trinitarian benediction that closes the epistle. On the threefold form, the grammatical voices note the three Persons. Barnes and Robertson note this famous benediction names the three Persons of the Godhead — "the Lord Jesus Christ," "God" (the Father), and "the Holy Ghost" — and invokes a distinct blessing from each; one of the clearest scriptural witnesses to the Trinity.

On "grace... love... communion," the witnesses note the three blessings. Barnes and Calvin note the order: "the grace (free favour) of the Lord Jesus Christ" (our access to the Father is through Christ's grace), "the love of God" (the Father's love, the fountain of all), and "the communion (fellowship, koinonia) of the Holy Ghost" (our partaking of the Spirit and his fellowship) — each Person's proper blessing.

On the doctrine, the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the trinitarian witness: the equal invocation of the three Persons together attests their distinct personhood and unity of Godhead; the benediction is a standing proof of the Trinity and a model of Christian blessing. The verse displays the trinitarian benediction that closes the epistle.

The witnesses agree: this famous benediction names the three Persons — "the Lord Jesus Christ," "God" (the Father), and "the Holy Ghost" — invoking a distinct blessing from each, one of the clearest scriptural witnesses to the Trinity (Barnes, Robertson); "the grace of Christ" (our access through his free favour), "the love of God" (the Father's love, the fountain), and "the communion of the Holy Ghost" (our fellowship in the Spirit), each Person's proper blessing (Barnes, Calvin); the equal invocation of the three attesting their distinct personhood and unity of Godhead (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the trinitarian benediction. The verse marks the three: Christ, God, the Spirit. The verse displays the blessings: grace, love, communion. The verse displays the doctrine: the Trinity. The verse displays the model: Christian blessing. The benediction displays the three Persons invoked in one blessing. The verse displays the grace: of Christ. The verse displays the love: of God. The verse displays the communion: of the Spirit. The trinitarian benediction that closes the epistle displays the crowning blessing and a great witness to the Trinity: Paul invokes the grace of Christ, the love of the Father, and the communion of the Holy Spirit upon them all — the three Persons named together, distinct yet one God, in the prayer that seals the letter. The verse displays the trinitarian grace that closes Second Corinthians, the model of all Christian benediction.
